--- Page 1 ---
THE ISALPROGRAM PROGRAMMING LANGUAGE
A PREPRINT
Ezequiel López-Rubio∗
Department of Computer Languages and Computer Science
University of Málaga
Bulevar Louis Pasteur, 35
29071 Málaga, Spain
ezeqlr@lcc.uma.es
May 19, 2026
ABSTRACT
We introduce IsalProgram (Instruction Set and Language for Programming), a novel assembly-like
programming language with three distinctive theoretical properties: (1) it is a regular language in the
sense of formal language theory, meaning its programs are accepted by a finite automaton; (2) every
finite string over the instruction alphabet is a syntactically valid program; and (3) it makes no explicit
use of memory addresses or variable names, absolute or relative. Programs are finite sequences of
tokens drawn from a fixed instruction set, and are executed on a virtual machine whose sole data
structure is a circular doubly linked list (CDLL) navigated by three data pointers, with control flow
governed by two code pointers. We give a complete formal definition of the language and its virtual
machine, prove its regularity, and demonstrate its expressive power. We further discuss IsalProgram’s
potential advantages as a target language for neural program synthesis, the amenability of its program
space to metric-based exploration via the Levenshtein edit distance, and directions for analysing
computability and complexity within this framework.
Keywords regular language · assembly language · circular doubly linked list · program synthesis · neural transformers ·
Levenshtein distance · formal language theory
1
Introduction
Most practical programming languages occupy the upper tiers of the Chomsky hierarchy [Hopcroft et al., 2006]: they
require context-free or even context-sensitive grammars, and their parsers must maintain a non-trivial state. This is a
reasonable price to pay for human readability, but it imposes costs when languages are used as objects of study rather
than as tools for human programmers—costs that manifest in program synthesis, automated program repair, and the
formal analysis of computability.
We present IsalProgram, a language that sits at the computational simplicity end of the human readability/computational
simplicity spectrum without sacrificing computational power. IsalProgram is:
• Regular. Its set of valid programs is a regular language. Any valid program is accepted by a trivial one-state
deterministic finite automaton (DFA) after a single left-to-right scan.
• Total. Every finite string over the instruction alphabet is a syntactically valid program. There are no parse
errors.
• Address-free. No variable names, constant memory addresses, labels, or numeric literals are embedded in the
instruction stream. All data live in a circular doubly linked list (CDLL) navigated by three pointer registers.
Despite its syntactic minimality, IsalProgram is computationally universal: we show in Section 6 that it can simulate a
Turing machine, placing it in the class of Turing-complete languages [Turing, 1936, Sipser, 2012].
∗Corresponding author. ITIS Software. Universidad de Málaga. C/ Arquitecto Francisco Peñalosa 18, 29010, Málaga, Spain
arXiv:2605.17008v1  [cs.PL]  16 May 2026


--- Page 2 ---
The IsalProgram Programming Language
A PREPRINT
The paper is organised as follows. Section 2 situates IsalProgram relative to existing work on minimal and esoteric
languages, neural program synthesis, and program metrics. Section 3 gives the formal definition of the instruction
set. Section 4 defines the virtual machine. Section 5 proves regularity. Section 6 establishes Turing completeness.
Section 7 explains how to develop functions in IsalProgram. Section 8 discusses theoretical and practical implications,
and Section 9 concludes.
2
Related Work
Minimal and esoteric languages.
The closest predecessor in spirit is Brainfuck [Müller, 1993], a Turing-complete
language with only eight instructions operating on a one-dimensional tape. Like IsalProgram, Brainfuck is Turing-
complete and address-free. Unlike IsalProgram, its set of syntactically valid programs is not a regular language because
matching bracket pairs requires a pushdown automaton [Sipser, 2012]. IsalProgram removes this restriction entirely:
its instruction set is flat and bracket-free, making syntax trivially checkable. The instruction set of IsalProgram is
much more powerful, so typical assembly-language-level calculations can be easily performed. Moreover, IsalProgram
supports data types and modularity via function calls.
Assembly and low-level languages.
Traditional assembly languages [Hyde, 2010, Patterson and Hennessy, 2021]
expose hardware registers and memory addresses directly. They require a two-pass assembler to resolve labels and are
not regular. IsalProgram replaces the address space with a dynamically-sized CDLL, eliminating the need for symbolic
addressing.
Neural program synthesis.
Recent large language models have demonstrated impressive code generation [Chen
et al., 2021, Austin et al., 2021] on languages such as Python and Java. However, the irregular syntax of such languages
requires the model to maintain complex structural constraints. A regular target language removes this burden: any token
sequence is valid, so the model need not learn to balance brackets, match types, or respect scoping rules [Vaswani et al.,
2017]. IsalProgram has been designed with this use-case in mind.
Metric spaces of programs.
The Levenshtein edit distance [Levenshtein, 1966] between two strings is a natural
measure of proximity in program space. For most languages, syntactic validity is not preserved under arbitrary single-
token edits, so the Levenshtein ball around a valid program often contains many invalid programs. Because IsalProgram
is total, every string in the Levenshtein ball of any IsalProgram program is itself a valid program—a property exploited
in evolutionary and distance-based program search [Koza, 1992, Navarro, 2001].
3
The IsalProgram Language
3.1
Alphabet and Programs
Let Σ denote the finite set of IsalProgram tokens (instructions). A program is any element of Σ∗(including the empty
program).
Definition 3.1 (IsalProgram program). An IsalProgram program is a finite sequence π = t1 t2 . . . tn with each ti ∈Σ.
The set of all programs is L = Σ∗.
3.2
Instruction Set
Table 1 lists all instructions together with their mnemonics. They are grouped into seven categories: flow control,
pointer movement (code and data), node operations, copy, arithmetic, string, and load.
3.3
Semantics of Error-Prone Instructions
Several instructions are defined to be no-ops on error rather than raising exceptions. Specifically:
• Ad: if the divisor (ternary node) is zero, do nothing.
• Aq: if the argument is negative, do nothing.
• Sc: if either operand is not a string, or the primary node is not a string, do nothing.
• Sx: if primary is not a string or secondary/ternary are not integers, do nothing; negative indices are treated as
in Python (counting from the end).
2


--- Page 3 ---
The IsalProgram Programming Language
A PREPRINT
Table 1: IsalProgram instruction set. Here x, y ∈{p, s, t} (primary, secondary, ternary) with x ̸= y.
Category
Mnemonic
Description
Flow control
J
Jump: set IP ←JP
Bp/Bs/Bt
Branch if datum at primary/secondary/ternary pointer is
non-zero (true); set IP ←JP
Kp/Ks/Kt
Function call: push IP onto the stack and jump to the
address stored at primary/secondary/ternary pointer
R
Function return: pop the return address from the top of
the stack to IP
H
Halt: stop execution
W
Wait: no operation
Pointer movement
Mxy
Move data pointer x to position of data pointer y
Mji
Move JP to current IP position
Np/Ns/Nt
Advance primary/secondary/ternary pointer to next CDLL
node
Pp/Ps/Pt
Retreat primary/secondary/ternary pointer to previous
CDLL node
Nj
Advance JP to next instruction
Pj
Retreat JP to previous instruction
Node operations
Ib/Ii/If/Is
Insert new Boolean/integer/float/string node after primary;
advance primary
D
Delete node at primary (no-op if CDLL has one node);
advance pointers that pointed to the deleted node
Copy
Cxy
Copy datum from node at x into node at y
Cjx
Copy JP into node at x
Cxj
If node at x contains a valid address then copy it into JP
Arithmetic
Aa/As/Am/Ad
Add/subtract/multiply/divide data at secondary and
ternary; store in primary
An
Negate datum at secondary; store in primary
Aq
Square root of datum at secondary; store in primary
String
Sc
Concatenate strings at secondary and ternary; store in
primary
Sx
Extract substring of primary using indices at secondary
(start) and ternary (end)
Load
Zp/Zs/Zt
Zero datum at primary/secondary/ternary
L1–L15
Load integer constant 1–15 into node at primary
Le/Lp
Load Euler’s number e / π into node at primary
• Cxy: types must be compatible; otherwise do nothing.
This design keeps every program semantically well-defined for all inputs, reinforcing the totality property.
4
The IsalProgram Virtual Machine
4.1
State
A virtual machine (VM) state is a tuple σ = (IP, JP, ST, CDLL, p, s, t) where:
• IP ∈{1, . . . , n + 1, halt} is the instruction pointer, with n the length of the program.
• JP ∈{1, . . . , n + 1, halt} is the jump pointer.
• ST is a stack of instruction positions.
• CDLL is a non-empty circular doubly linked list of typed nodes. Each node v carries a datum d(v) ∈
B ∪Z ∪R ∪Σ∗and a type τ(v) ∈{bool, int, float, string}.
• p, s, t are the primary, secondary, and ternary data pointers, each pointing to a node in the CDLL.
3


--- Page 4 ---
The IsalProgram Programming Language
A PREPRINT
v1
v2
v3
v4
next
next
next
next
prev
prev
prev
prev
p
s
t
p: primary
s: secondary
t: ternary
Figure 1: Circular doubly linked list (CDLL) with four nodes. The three data pointers (p, s, t) can point to arbitrary
nodes independently. The list wraps around: the successor of v4 is v1 and the predecessor of v1 is v4.
4.2
Initialisation
Given a program π = t1 . . . tn and a non-empty input list ⟨v1, . . . , vk⟩of typed values with k > 0:
1. Set ST to the empty stack.
2. Construct a CDLL with nodes m1, . . . , mk in order, linked circularly.
3. If the program is empty (n = 0), then set IP = halt, JP = halt. Otherwise, set IP = 1, JP = 1.
4. Set p = s = t = m1.
4.3
Execution
Execution proceeds as follows. While IP ̸= halt:
1. Fetch instruction tIP.
2. Execute the instruction (see Table 1 and Section 3.2).
3. Unless the instruction explicitly modifies IP, set IP ←IP + 1.
4. If IP > n, execution terminates (implicit halt).
The output of the program is the sequence of data values currently stored in the CDLL at termination, starting from the
primary pointer p to the next nodes.
4.4
The CDLL
Figure 1 illustrates the CDLL structure with three data pointers and the relationship between node operations.
4.5
Pointer Architecture
Figure 2 shows the full pointer architecture of the VM, including both the code pointers (IP and JP) and the data pointers
(p, s, t).
5
Regularity of IsalProgram
Theorem 5.1. The set of IsalProgram programs L = Σ∗is a regular language.
Proof. By definition, L = Σ∗is the Kleene closure of the finite alphabet Σ [Kleene, 1956]. The language Σ∗is
accepted by the one-state DFA M = ({q0}, Σ, δ, q0, {q0}) where δ(q0, a) = q0 for all a ∈Σ. Since M is a DFA, L is
regular.
The key design decision that enables Theorem 5.1 is the absence of any bracket-matching, label-resolution, or type-
annotation requirements: there is simply nothing to check. Figure 3 depicts the minimal DFA for L.
4


--- Page 5 ---
The IsalProgram Programming Language
A PREPRINT
Instruction sequence π
Mji
Np
Bp
Nj
J
Aa
H
· · ·
IP
JP
CDLL
v1
v2
v3
v4
· · ·
p
s
t
Figure 2: Full pointer architecture of the IsalProgram VM. Two instruction-level pointers (IP and JP) index into the
program sequence; three data-level pointers (p, s, t) index into the CDLL.
q0
a ∈Σ
Figure 3: Minimal DFA for L = Σ∗. The single state q0 is both the start state and the unique accepting state. Every
token loops back to q0.
Corollary 5.2. Every finite string over Σ is a syntactically valid IsalProgram program.
Corollary 5.3. The set of IsalProgram programs of length at most n has cardinality Pn
k=0 |Σ|k.
6
Computational Universality
Although IsalProgram has a trivially simple syntax, its virtual machine is computationally universal.
Theorem 6.1. IsalProgram is Turing-complete.
Proof sketch. We simulate a two-tape Turing machine (TM) [Turing, 1936, Minsky, 1967]. The CDLL models both
tapes concatenated: the primary pointer acts as the TM’s head, and the secondary pointer marks the boundary between
the two tapes. The ternary pointer holds the current state index as an integer.
Tape operations. Np/Pp move the head right/left. Ii/If/Is extend the tape on demand (simulating the infinite tape). D
contracts the tape, which is not required to simulate the infinite tape but could be employed to reduce the number of
nodes in the CDLL.
State transitions. Each TM transition δ(q, a) = (q′, a′, d) is encoded as a fixed-length IsalProgram subprogram that (i)
tests the symbol at the head using Bp/Bs/Bt, (ii) writes the new symbol with a copy instruction, and (iii) updates the
state register and moves the head.
Control flow. The Kx/R mechanism replaces the standard dispatch table: Kx saves the return address and performs the
call; R returns from the call.
Since every TM can be encoded in this way, and IsalProgram programs can be executed on the IsalProgram VM,
IsalProgram is Turing-complete [Sipser, 2012].
The tension between Theorem 5.1 and Theorem 6.1 is only apparent: regularity is a property of the syntax (the set of
strings), while Turing-completeness is a property of the semantics (what the programs compute). As Müller [1993]
demonstrated, a language with a trivially simple syntax can still be Turing-complete.
5


--- Page 6 ---
The IsalProgram Programming Language
A PREPRINT
Caller sets up
args and function address
Caller stores function address
→node at x
Kx
Call the function at x
Function body
(uses tmp nodes)
Delete tmp nodes
store results
R
return to caller
Caller resumes
stack top =
return address
Figure 4: Function call protocol in IsalProgram. Kx saves the return address into the stack, and jumps to the function
whose address is stored at the node pointed by x. After the function has completed its calculations, a final R at the end
of the function body returns control to the caller.
7
Functions
IsalProgram supports a structured notion of functions that avoids any literal memory address constant in the code. A
function call works as follows:
1. The caller ensures that the primary pointer and the subsequent CDLL nodes hold the function’s input arguments.
2. The function body inserts temporary nodes for local computation immediately after those input nodes.
3. Upon return, the function deletes all temporary nodes and stores its results into the primary pointer and the
subsequent CDLL nodes.
Figure 4 illustrates the function call protocol. The call/return mechanism uses the Kx/R pair: Kx pushes the current
IP to the stack and jumps to the function at the address stored in the node pointed by x, and a final R at the end of the
function body returns control to the address retrieved from the stack. This is analogous to a call/ret pair in assembly,
without any function address specification in the code.
8
Discussion
8.1
IsalProgram as a Target for Neural Program Synthesis
Recent work has shown that large language models (LLMs) trained on human-written code [Chen et al., 2021, Austin
et al., 2021] can synthesise programs from natural-language descriptions. However, all languages studied so far have
complex, non-regular syntax: the model must simultaneously learn both the meaning and the form of programs.
IsalProgram separates these concerns. Because L = Σ∗, the model’s output is always syntactically valid; no post-
generation repair or filtering is needed. The model can concentrate entirely on the semantics—the what-to-compute
problem.
6


--- Page 7 ---
The IsalProgram Programming Language
A PREPRINT
Formally, for a model parameterised by θ that generates a sequence of tokens t1, . . . , tn via
pθ(π) =
n
Y
i=1
pθ(ti | t1, . . . , ti−1),
(1)
validity is guaranteed for all θ and all n, which simplifies training objectives and evaluation [Vaswani et al., 2017].
8.2
The Levenshtein Metric on Program Space
The edit distance [Levenshtein, 1966] between two programs π, π′ is
d(π, π′) = ed(π, π′),
(2)
the minimum number of single-token insertions, deletions, and substitutions needed to transform π into π′. The totality
of IsalProgram means the shortest path between two programs according to the edit distance is composed of valid
programs.
This makes IsalProgram amenable to nearest-neighbour program search, genetic programming [Koza, 1992], and
mutation-based automatic program repair: any mutant is a valid program.
8.3
Computability and Complexity Analysis
Standard computability arguments are developed with respect to Turing machines [Turing, 1936, Sipser, 2012]. The
IsalProgram VM provides an alternative, arguably more natural, model: programs are finite strings rather than state-
transition tables, and the memory model (CDLL) is more structured than a tape but more flexible than a fixed-size
register file.
We conjecture that the standard time and space complexity classes (PTIME, PSPACE, etc. [Papadimitriou, 1994])
can be characterised cleanly in terms of the number of CDLL operations and the maximum CDLL size, respectively.
Formalising this connection is left as future work.
8.4
Limitations
Three-pointer constraint.
The three data pointers impose a strict register-pressure cost: some computations require
careful sequencing of pointer-move instructions that would be trivial with four or more pointers. Extending the VM to
four or more pointers while preserving regularity is straightforward (since regularity depends only on the absence of
syntax constraints, not on the number of pointers).
No immediate literals.
IsalProgram can load only the constants 0, 1–15, e, and π directly. Larger constants must be
composed arithmetically. This is a feature—it keeps the alphabet finite—but it makes some programs verbose.
Halting problem.
As with all Turing-complete languages, the halting problem for IsalProgram is undecidable [Turing,
1936].
9
Conclusion
We have introduced IsalProgram, a novel programming language with three remarkable properties that coexist in a single
design: (1) its syntax is a regular language (in fact Σ∗), (2) every string is a valid program, and (3) it is Turing-complete
despite using no variable names or literal memory addresses.
We have given a precise formal specification of the instruction set and virtual machine, proved regularity and Turing-
completeness, and developed a function call protocol.
The language opens three concrete directions for future research. First, the use of IsalProgram as a target language for
neural program synthesis, where the totality of the language eliminates the syntactic validity constraint on generated
sequences. Second, the exploitation of the Levenshtein metric over Σ∗for evolutionary or distance-based program
search. Third, the development of a complexity theory grounded in CDLL operations rather than Turing machine steps.
We believe IsalProgram offers a uniquely clean theoretical laboratory for studying the relationship between syntax,
semantics, and computation.
7


--- Page 8 ---
The IsalProgram Programming Language
A PREPRINT
